sentences.text: He said he would go to my parents, and he would see what was going on.
So when he went to my parents, of course, my dad told him that he had given a big sum of money to the Germans, that we would be going to Portugal.
So he-- when he saw us on the list, and one day the head in the barracks-- we were in barracks-- came in and they said you're-- you're going on transport.
I quickly contacted my husband.
I said you better go to Meno-- that was his first name, Meno DeHaan-- and tell him we are on the list and he better get us off, otherwise we'll be leaving.
And my husband went to him.
He said, don't worry, you will be off the list.
And indeed, that was true.
He took us off the list and we did not go to Auschwitz that day.
Otherwise, we were gone.
After a week we would have been in Auschwitz.
Then the day later, they came from the head of the Germans and they came to me and they said, you can leave the camp.
And they gave us 10 guilder, at that time, to pay for our fare to Amsterdam.
But it was a long walk to the train station, at least an hour walk, at least, and with the young child.
But at that time we met a gentleman, a non-Jewish man with his car.
He was, I think, selling merchandise to the people in the camp, and he recognized my husband through his twin brother.
And he said, are you so-and-so?
He said, yes.
He said, yeah, I know your brother well.
Yeah, I said, yes, but we have to go to Hochhalen, now I remember.
That was far from Westerbork.
And he said, yes, from Hochhalen you can get the train to Amsterdam.
I said, yes, but we have to get there.
He said, well, I will smuggle you in my car and take you to Hochhalen.
And he took us to a lady that lived opposite the train station.
There were two sisters.
Their name was Van Dam.
And she said, what can I do?
Well, I had not eaten the whole week that I was in the camp.
I just, I could not eat.
I was so in bad shape.
Did they give you food?
And I said-- well, you could get something, but I-- I couldn't eat it.
And then my-- this lady said, what would you like?
I said, if you have a cup of tea with a-- a slice of bread, it's gonna be dry, as long as it's one slice of bread, I will be satisfied.
And she said, oh, yes, I will give you that.
And she fed us.
And then when the train-- they announced the train is there and we said goodbye to them and we went to the train-- because we had the money that they had given us.
Because they took even my wedding ring away when we came, but they gave it back to me.
And--
In the camp, the Germans.
When we arrived, they took everything away, yes.
They gave me my wedding ring back and I still have that.
And then, we went on the train and we came to Amsterdam.
And we heard then that all the women and a lot of men were picked up before that to go in working camps.
They had to work.
And all the women were alone at home.
They picked up all these women, which were also relatives of mine, and they put them in camps.
And there were other camps, not only Westerbork but other camps.
And, well, we came home, the first thing I did, I called at my mother's place and my mother said, oh, my God, it's Sophie.
And her husband.
And they took me in, and they had my keys.
And one neighbor said, what a shame that you came back.
You had the most beautiful stove.
My eye-- I know that stove I could have used so well.
It was a neighbor.
I did not think at the time that there were-- everybody is without.
They have nothing.
So anything is better than nothing.
But my-- my-- I had good stuff, you know?
So naturally, everybody was out to get the best they could, if they-- if they could.
Now this was 1942?
Was a week.
One week.
One week.
We came back the 1st of October.
And in the meantime, our business was taken over by the Germans.
They-- they threw my dad out and they were running the business.
So when I came back, I went to this man, the owner, and I said, can I get a job here? 'Cause I was so used to it.
And they said, oh, we'd love to have you, and-- because you know the business.
And I said, yes.
And I said, but you know, the salary is very low.
See, we took money out.
The salary is, very low.
I need a little more salary because everything has become so expensive and I have a young child and she would like an egg once in a while, and they are impossible to get.
And he said, yes, I will give you a little more, but keep your mouth shut.
Don't talk to the others about it.
You will get a little more than the others.
And that's what he did.
But we could-- people came in and asked if they had butter or they had something that they wanted to give me to get leather, because the shoes were worn out.
And I used to whisper to them, come back after 5:00, because then she's gone-- the boss's wife-- and they'll be gone.
And I had a key to the store.
As a matter of fact, the head of the National Cash Register gave me a key to the National Cash Register, which I was not allowed to have even, because they had them all.
But he gave me that on the QT that I could see what was taken in every day.
That was quite something.
I was back in my own home.
Working in the store, [INAUDIBLE] in the business.
All there.
They were all home.
They were all in there, yes.
No, no, I never-- I never thought I would be taken again because we had paid so much money, we thought we would be transport to Portugal one day.
Never, never.
And then one day, a big car came and they took us again, and they took us to a theater in Amsterdam, and we were put in there.
My husband, my daughter, my parents.
All of us, the whole family.
My sisters, everybody was taken.
And-- but only for one night because the next day we were let out.
And that, the boss that took our store never thought that we would come out that easily.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] But because of the money we had given them, they really did that.
And then-- and we knew a lot of people.
See, we were in business, so we knew a lot of people.
And then my brother knew the head of this theater and the head said, can you get some gin, you know, bottles of gin?
And I'll give that.
If you can get me two bottles, they will take that as a bribe.
Now get your family out.
And my brother did that.
He could get what he wanted.
He was an Englishman, and he was not married.
So he was without a star, even-- he never wore a star.
So, but he was British.
So he gave them the gin and they let us free.
And we went back to the store, to home again.
Were you aware at this point of Auschwitz and what was happening to other Jews?
No, never knew.
Never, never, never knew.
We-- I-- really, we didn't know anything until we came to France.
Even in Westerbork, we didn't know.
People went there and we thought-- because my mother asked once, the German, what must they do with a woman my age?
What are they gonna do with that?
Put me to work?
And they said, yes, we can use all workers no matter what age.
And what did we know?
We didn't know of anything else.
It was not known at that time that there were extermination camps.
But once then, in '43, when we came to France, there we met people from Poland, from East Europe, and they told us about the camps.
And before that time we did not know anything about it.
This was in 1943 you made a trip to France?
We were in a camp again and the end of '43, they sent us to France, because of-- I-- I was British by birth, of course.
And I said, my husband was stateless, he had no-- no nationality at all.
I was-- we were told that by a German Jew that worked for the-- they had to work for the Nazis, of course, in the camp, in Westerbork.
And they said, you're stateless-- just say that.
And so because my husband was stateless and I was British, they said-- the German woman that spoke to me said, I was sent-- where are your papers?
I said, well, when they picked us up, I didn't go to my bureau to get my papers, which I should have done, after all.
I didn't have them anymore.
But I left them, of course, at home.
And she said, well, I will send you to France, but if they're going to throw you out, then I have nothing to do with it.
I said, OK, we have to take that chance.
In the meantime, my husband was very shaky.
He had a-- an ear, a mastoid, isn't that called a mastoid?
And so, when we came to France, they called out, the man with the one ear, and they put him in a hospital there in Vittel.
That was the first place we came.
And I remember my husband saying they were French nurses.
They were also captured, even though they were Gentiles.
But they were from English descent, and they were not allowed to be there.
And they-- and they were not Jewish.
And they asked my father, [FRENCH].
And he thought they were saying, do you have pain?
He didn't know that pain was also bread.
So he said, no, he had no pain.
So I went there and I said, what do you mean by giving everybody bread and nothing to my husband?
Well, then they told me.
He did not understand, of course.
And I said, well, he needs some bread, too, the same as anybody.
Now this was in a hospital?
It was a hospital in the camp, that they made a hospital in France.
Well, that was from Holland.
We came right-- in Westerbork.
Yes, they picked us up again and they put us in the camp again.
And from Westerbork, there they asked us our nationality and everything and then we got to the point that we could go to France.
Oh, quite a few months.
Yes.
Terrible, terrible.
We were full of lice, and food was terrible, but you had to eat it because to survive, otherwise, you would die.
My daughter was with me, yes.
And--
We were in-- we had the barracks.
There were three mattresses on top of each other.
That's how the sleeping conditions were there.
No, no, not mistreated, no.
My sisters were in a different barrack.
One sister was in my barrack and my sister was in a different barrack.
And my-- my mother went into hiding after we were taken.
My father was taken also to the camp.
And he was first taken to prison.
They had said he'd done some illegal things with money-- which was not true, of course.
They just said something to get you.
So he was in prison, came out of prison, went into the barrack that's special for people that had been to prison.
And those, they sent right away to Auschwitz, if possible.
But also, because we knew so many people, and we knew the head of the barrack, we said, can you do something to keep my dad from going to Auschwitz?
He said, yes, I will see to it.
And my mother, after we were all gone, there was a friend of ours, she was from a mixed marriage, half Jewish, half-- her father was Jewish, her mother was Gentile.
She went to my mother and put my mother into hiding somewhere.
But my mother got very sick there because she couldn't stand being away from her children and her husband.
That-- and then when we knew we would go to France, my sister went up to the head of the camp and said, we'll get my mother back here.
So we quickly got hold of this Gentile girl and she got my mother back into the camp.
Well, we could send a letter.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Yes.
You could send mail.
Yes.
My husband was making the roads.
He had to work as a road worker, and I was doing-- tearing clothes apart because everything they could use.
Later, my husband was a tailor and he had to repair the German, their wives' clothes or make them over.
And my daughter was walking around and then she got, my mother made her like a nurse.
She made her a little apron from rags and, you know, like she was a Red Cross nurse.
She was only a child, but the people did remember her very well because [INTERPOSING VOICES] she was such a beauty when she was little.
Well, you were always afraid that you would be on the next transport going around.
You never knew if you were not one of the 1,000 that would go.
Yeah.
Yeah, because the head of the workshop came by and he said, get more work done.
And a friend of mine that was next to me, she said, tell him you have a better paper than he has because you're not going to go to Auschwitz.
That's what she said.
I said, I'm not talking about that.
I'll just let things happen what happens.
I am a fatalist, actually, by nature.
What's going to happen, I can't-- I can't do anything about that.
But then when we were called to the-- what they called at that time, the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I went there, and they asked me who I am, and I said who I was, and that I was born in England and that I was British by birth.
And they said, and what about your husband?
I said, well, he is stateless.
That's how we came to go to France.
But once in France, there was an English lady and she went to interview my husband.
My husband was sick at that time.
He was in the hospital.
So when I visited my husband in the afternoon, we called her 222, that was the number she lived.
He said, 222 was here and wants to know all everything about my family, [BACKGROUND NOISE] and how come I'm in the camp.
I said, well, when I see her I will talk to her and tell her if she wants to know anything she should come to me, that you do not understand English at all.
And I met her and she came.
And she said, what is this with your husband?
I said, my husband's father was born in England so he is from British descent.
And when was he born?
So I gave some date.
I didn't know the date.
And I gave a date and I gave a place in England and she took it all down.
Of course, had the war taken longer, I would have been thrown out of that place, [LAUGHS] all of us, you know, because nothing was true what I said.
But I had to say something to get her off my husband's back.
So now you were together in Vittel.
In Vittel, we were together.
The end of 1943.
My husband and my daughter.
Also in Vittel.
And my-- my one brother was living in England.
He was in-- he was a RAF, with the RAF.
He was living in England.
And my other brother, as a Britisher, was sent to a place in Poland in a camp, but it was not in a-- a-- a-- it was an internment camp.
Because one of the Nazis said to him, I'll exchange places with you anytime because [INTERPOSING VOICES].
Yes, yes, that was very important.
They were all, except one sister was born in Holland, and that's why she was sent to Bergen-Belsen with her husband.
Because she had a passport with her, a Dutch passport.
And that was-- we never said that my husband was her brother.
We never said that.
You know, we-- we kept everything on the QT.
In France, we were liberated in '44 because Paris was liberated earlier than Holland.
And we went from one camp to another camp.
The Americans.
I remember them very well.
They were the nicest people I had ever met, the nicest soldiers.
They were so kind to us, and especially to my daughter.
Because there was one soldier who said, I have a little girl like that, and I miss her lots, that I'm not with her.
And then he said, when are you leaving?
I said, yes, as soon as they tell us we can leave, we will go back to Holland.
But the British people came over and they said, you and your daughter can go to England right away, but not your husband because your husband is not British.
And I said, no, I'm not going without my husband anywhere.
We went through so much that I was certainly not going to England now and leave my husband alone.
We will just wait and take it as it comes.
And then my husband was working as a tailor for the Americans, and they wanted him to go around wherever they went because they liked his work.
He was so precise and neat with everything.
So they wanted him to go along and-- with them.
After the liberation.
We were still in Vittel when this happened.
Then they sent us to [FRENCH], which happened to be a-- a land of Maurice Chevalier.
And they put us there.
Not that he was good-- he was not good, either, but they-- they just took it, of course.
Like the Germans took from us, the British-- the Americans took from them.
It was a big estate and we were there.
And--
My whole family and so many others, a lot of people.
Not us-- and we were not the only ones.
There were a lot of Gentiles, and there was a little anti-Semitism in there.
They-- they always said to me you speak much nicer than a lot of-- a lot of people had the accent, you know, that Jewish accent.
And that they did not like, you know, the real English, the real, real English Gentiles.
And they used to say, yes, your English is so nice and very refined.
And I said, well, this is the way I always spoke.
And-- because we had to use-- we could use the-- the kitchens of the hotels.
See, the hotels were taken for us, that we could stay somewhere.
There was not always space for us.
So one was in a-- in this hotel, the other in that hotel.
We were not always together.
And my husband was with me and my daughter, but my other family were in different hotels.
Yes, we could go outside but not we were not free to walk around because they was-- always the Germans were the boss there.
And one day, my husband, of course, he had the-- with his ear, he had to go to the hospital in Nancy, N-A-N-C-Y, Nancy.
And going there, they operated on him and he walked around there.
And there was a prison near the hospital, and one of the men that was in the hospital said, there's a Dutch woman in the prison.
And there was, down below-- and my husband walked by and said, in Dutch, are you Dutch?
Are you Dutch?
And he found the woman and--
